nativeRange.habitat: The Bronze Corydoras inhabits quiet, shallow freshwaters with soft substrates and is also found in flowing streams. It is a bottom-dwelling species that typically moves and rests in schools near the substrate.

diet.notes: Accepts sinking pellets and wafers, frozen or live bloodworms, tubifex, brine shrimp, and blanched vegetables. Requires regular targeted feeding — tends not to thrive on leftover food from tankmates alone. Benefits from variety in the diet.

avoidWith: predatory fish that can swallow them
fish requiring salt or high hardness

imageCandidates.notes: Juan Carlos Palau D; 640x429. Group of four bronze corys resting along the substrate, showing the diagnostic olive-gold iridescent lateral stripe on live vibrant individuals; group shot best represents this schooling species.
Andrew Keller; 1110x832. Tack-sharp single bronze specimen in a planted tank with clear green-teal iridescent flank; CC0, highest image quality of the pool.
Gabriel Resende Veiga; 4231x2821. Group of the selectively-bred Albino form (pink/cream body, red eyes) feeding, documenting the second commercial color variant noted in careNotes.

summary: The Bronze Corydoras is widely regarded as one of the most popular and beginner-friendly catfish in the freshwater hobby. Peaceful and sociable, it tends to thrive in groups of six or more and is sold in both its wild-type bronze form and a selectively bred Albino variant.
careNotes:
Generally considered one of the best beginner fish in the hobby due to its hardiness and tolerance of a wide parameter range. Two commercially available color forms exist: the wild-type bronze (olive-gold iridescent) and the Albino (pink/cream body with red eyes) — the albino is a selectively bred genetic variant of the same species, not a separate species, and has no additional health concerns. Both forms have the same care requirements and can be kept together.

Substrate is critical: smooth sand is strongly preferred over gravel. Sharp or coarse gravel tends to damage the sensitive barbels (whiskers), which can lead to infection and decline. A minimum of 3 cm of sand depth is recommended.

Corydoras aeneus is a facultative air-breather and will occasionally dash to the surface to gulp air — this is normal behavior and not a sign of distress, though persistent surface gulping in well-oxygenated water may indicate gill irritation.

The pectoral fin spines are sharp and can pierce skin when handling; use a soft net and handle with care. These spines can also become entangled in nets.

Copper-based medications (commonly used for ich and parasites) should be avoided — this species, like most scaled catfish, has been documented as sensitive to copper at therapeutic doses. Use copper-free alternatives.

Likes to school tightly, especially when newly introduced or if feeling threatened. Solo specimens or very small groups tend to become reclusive and stressed; groups of 6 or more are strongly advised. A school of 6 in a 75L tank is a reasonable minimum; larger groups in larger tanks are generally preferred.
breedingNotes: Breeding is generally considered moderate difficulty and can be achieved in home aquaria. Condition the group with high-quality live or frozen foods (bloodworms, tubifex). Spawning is typically triggered by a large (50–70%) water change using cooler water (a few degrees below tank temperature), mimicking seasonal rainfall patterns. A ratio of 2 males per female is commonly recommended. The characteristic 'T-position' mating is distinctive: the male grasps the female's barbels with his pectoral fins while she collects sperm in a cupped ventral fin pouch, then deposits eggs on glass, plants, or other surfaces. Eggs hatch in approximately 3–5 days depending on temperature. Fry can be fed microworms and baby brine shrimp once the yolk sac is absorbed. Adults may eat eggs if not removed.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Primary source used in place of Aquarium Co-Op (which returned 404 for the species-specific URL). SeriouslyFish is the slice's first listed secondary and is a well-regarded specialist reference for freshwater fish.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Care guide blog post covering Corydoras general care and C. aeneus specifically. Confirmed tank size (20 gal / 76L), group size (6+), diet, temperament, and substrate preferences.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed max size 7.5 cm SL, temperature 25–28°C, pH 6.0–8.0, hardness 5–19 dH, native range (Colombia and Trinidad to La Plata basin east of Andes), family Callichthyidae, order Siluriformes.

fish.breedingNotes: The Bronze Corydoras is an egg-layer that deposits its eggs on glass and plant surfaces. Spawning is typically triggered by cool water changes, and pairs engage in the characteristic 'T-position' mating embrace. Breeding is generally considered moderate difficulty in the home aquarium.
